serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Sirap Limau is approximately 250 ml (one glass), providing about 112 kcal, 28 g of carbohydrates, and 26 g of sugar.

nutrient_source.title: Energy and Nutrient Composition of Foods — Singapore HPB / IMR Malaysia MyFCD reference for sweetened beverages; closest analogous entry: rose syrup drink / calamansi lime drink
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) and IMR Malaysia MyFCD
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single exact entry for Sirap Limau exists in HPB or MyFCD databases. Values are adapted from HPB Singapore sweetened beverage entries and IMR Malaysia MyFCD rose syrup and calamansi lime juice component data. Input record values (energy 45 kcal, carbohydrate 11.2 g, sugar 10.5 g per 100 g) are consistent with a diluted rose syrup and calamansi lime drink at typical preparation ratios and are retained. Saturated fat estimated at 0.0 g given negligible total fat (0.05 g per 100 g). Monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat, cholesterol, and water set to -1 as detailed fatty acid and moisture data are not available in HPB or MyFCD for this beverage.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — sucrose and sugar-sweetened beverages analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Sirap Limau exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 63 based on the GI of sucrose (GI=65, University of Sydney GI Database) and sugar-sweetened beverages (GI range 58–68). The primary carbohydrate source is refined sucrose from rose syrup. Glycemic load per 250 ml serving calculated as: (63 × 28 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 17.6. This is consistent with a medium-GI, moderate-GL beverage.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (5 mg per 100 g; 12.5 mg per 250 ml serving)
hypertension.guidance: Sirap Limau is very low in sodium and does not pose a meaningful hypertensive risk; however, individuals should be mindful of total caloric and sugar intake when consuming sweetened beverages regularly.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined sugar content (10.5 g sugar per 100 g; approximately 26 g per 250 ml serving) from rose syrup, with a medium glycemic index of 63 and glycemic load of 17.6 per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit Sirap Limau due to its high refined sugar content and moderate glycemic load, which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; a sugar-free or reduced-sugar version using artificial sweeteners is a safer alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High added sugar intake from rose syrup is associated with elevated triglycerides and increased cardiovascular risk when consumed habitually
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit Sirap Limau to occasional consumption, as habitual intake of high-sugar beverages is linked to dyslipidaemia and increased cardiovascular risk, despite the beverage being very low in fat and sodium.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sirap Limau is a popular Malay sweet beverage widely consumed in Malaysia and Singapore. It is made by combining rose-flavoured syrup (Sirap), which is a bright red, highly sweetened cordial, with freshly squeezed calamansi lime juice (Limau Kasturi), a small, intensely sour Southeast Asian citrus fruit. The drink is always served chilled over ice, producing a vibrant red, sweet, and tangy beverage with a delicate floral aroma from the rose syrup and a refreshing citrus tartness from the calamansi. Per 100 g, Sirap Limau provides approximately 45 kcal, 0.1 g protein, 0.05 g fat, and 11.2 g carbohydrates of which 10.5 g is sugar, making it a high-sugar, low-fat, low-sodium beverage. A standard 250 ml serving delivers approximately 112 kcal and 26 g of sugar. It has a medium glycemic index (estimated GI 63) and a glycemic load of approximately 17.6 per serving, making it unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or those managing blood glucose. The drink is a cultural staple at Malay weddings, Ramadan iftar gatherings, and as a cooling accompaniment to spicy meals. It is vegetarian, vegan, halal, and gluten-free. The vitamin C contribution from calamansi lime juice (approximately 8.5 mg per 100 g) provides a modest antioxidant benefit, but the dominant nutritional concern is the high refined sugar content from the rose syrup.

confidence_reason: No single exact entry for Sirap Limau exists in HPB Singapore or IMR Malaysia MyFCD databases. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB sweetened beverage entries and MyFCD rose syrup and calamansi lime component data, and are consistent with the input record values. GI is estimated by analogy from sucrose and sugar-sweetened beverage GI data in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g is set to 0.0 g as total fat is only 0.05 g per 100 g; saturated fat is effectively trace/negligible and confirmed consistent with HPB sweetened beverage data. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions and moisture data for Sirap Limau are not available in HPB Singapore or IMR Malaysia MyFCD databases as of 2024. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 and vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: these are naturally zero as Sirap Limau is a plant-based sugar syrup and citrus juice beverage containing no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero per HPB beverage composition data. 4. GI of 63 is an analogous estimate based on sucrose GI (65) and sugar-sweetened beverage GI range (58–68) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Sirap Limau was found. 5. Ontology mapped to FoodOn broader class 'beverage food product' and FoodEx2 A0CKP 'Non-alcoholic flavoured drinks' as no exact codes exist for this specific beverage. 6. Multilingual names for Chinese, Tamil, and Hindi are provided as this is a Singaporean/Malaysian Malay dish consumed widely in Singapore, satisfying the Singaporean dish multilingual requirement.
